serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried pork tenderloin is approximately 150 g, representing a single-person main dish portion as commonly served in Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: 宫廷抓炒里脊 Stir-Fried Pork Tenderloin — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 宫廷抓炒里脊 Stir-Fried Pork Tenderloin (cr_id G00010146). Carbohydrate is recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source, which likely reflects the meat-only analytical fraction; the actual dish contains batter and sauce carbohydrates not captured in this entry. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Composite Foods — Analogous Estimate Based on Sweet and Sour Pork (Battered, Deep-Fried, Sauce-Coated)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 宫廷抓炒里脊. The HPB-recorded carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g (meat-only analytical fraction), making a direct GL calculation yield 0. However, the actual dish contains cornstarch batter and sweet sauce, contributing meaningful carbohydrates. GI is estimated at 55 (low) by analogy with battered and sauce-coated pork dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the 50–65 range for similar deep-fried battered meat preparations with sweet sauces. Glycemic load per serving is recorded as 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g × 150 g serving; this underestimates true GL due to the analytical limitation noted above. Consumers and clinicians should treat the actual GL as moderate given the batter and sauce components.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g threshold; potassium at 47 mg/100g provides negligible offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish or request a significantly reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium — more than two-thirds of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Cornstarch batter and sweet sauce contribute significant refined carbohydrates and added sugar not captured in the HPB 0 g/100g carbohydrate figure; deep-frying raises the overall glycaemic and caloric load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this dish because the cornstarch batter and sweet sauce substantially raise the glycaemic load beyond what the recorded 0 g carbohydrate suggests; if consumed, request minimal batter and reduced-sugar sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in oil raises total fat to 10.5 g/100g with likely significant saturated fat; sodium at 1050 mg/100g contributes to arterial stiffness and blood pressure elevation; cholesterol at 89 mg/100g adds further cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of high sodium (1050 mg/100g), elevated cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and deep-fried fat content, all of which adversely affect blood pressure, lipid profiles, and endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); pork delivers arachidonic acid, both amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and pork provides arachidonic acid; a steamed or braised pork tenderloin preparation is a lower-AGE alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 宫廷抓炒里脊 (Gong Ting Zhua Chao Li Ji), translated as Stir-Fried Pork Tenderloin or Imperial Court Crispy Pork Tenderloin, is a classic Chinese dish originating from Beijing imperial court cuisine. The dish is prepared by slicing pork tenderloin into thin strips or medallions, coating them in a seasoned cornstarch-and-egg batter, deep-frying until golden and crispy, and then tossing the fried pieces in a glossy sweet-and-sour sauce made from soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and aromatics. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, tender and juicy interior, and a balanced sour-sweet flavour profile. Per 100 g as recorded by the HPB Singapore Foodo database, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate — the latter reflecting the meat-only analytical fraction and not the full dish carbohydrate contribution from batter and sauce. Sodium is notably high at 1050 mg/100g due to soy sauce in the sauce base. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g from the pork and egg batter. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (batter and sauce raise glycaemic load), cardiovascular disease (high sodium, fat, and cholesterol), and rheumatoid arthritis (deep-frying AGEs and pork arachidonic acid). It is served as a main dish at lunch or dinner in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and is a popular item in Singaporean Chinese cuisine menus.
